subject: [DCAEGEN2] Use common secret template for cloudify password
commit_message:
[DCAEGEN2] Use common secret template for cloudify password

Link all references to cloudify password to ensure that it can be
automatically generated.
Set generated password strength to "basic" to work around
problem with Cloudify (DCAEGEN2-2450).

Issue-ID: DCAEGEN2-1975
Issue-ID: DCAEGEN2-2450
Change-Id: I53e154de854d33be686e830be8a827741bb3ec7e
Signed-off-by: Schmalzried, Terry (ts862m) <ts862m@att.com>
Signed-off-by: Krzysztof Opasiak <k.opasiak@samsung.com>
Signed-off-by: vv770d <vv770d@att.com>

> > I hope that this simple example will help you to understand the common secret template
> 
> My stack overflowed.  I don't understand where the value of the global secret gets initialized and why it doesn't get changed every time a component deploys (which would be bad).

This is because we use the concept of "masterPassword" (see https://masterpassword.app/masterpassword-algorithm.pdf).

In short. Every deployment with the same master password will have the same passwords generated. This allows us to increase security (because everyone may use different masterPassword for his or her deployment) but in the same time we get reproducibility.

> Also don't know why all the secret(s).yaml files include common.secretFast now

This is the heart of our solution that handles all of the magic. So instead of manually creating secrets you just include this one and fill in "secrets" section in you values.yaml.

Based on this common.secretFast will create kubernetes secrets. Additionally we support concept of "externalSecret" or "bring your own secret" in other words. This means that if "externalSecret" field is evaluated to non-empty string, the component will not create a new secret but instead reference the one that is already existing. And this is actually heavily used here.

So the secret is really created in the top most directory. As "password" field is never set here the actual password is automatically generated by our template using master password algorithm.

Then we pass the name of that secret to all submodules that are using it. In those modules becuase "externalSecret" would evaluate to non-empty string they will not create new secrets but rather reuse the one that is comming from the parent chart

> and whether that causes collisions when some included it previously (I assume for different secrets).

This should be included only once per component, otherwise the deployment will fail

I notice that policy, sdc, and dmaap are all failing at the Helm deploy stage.  Because of that, DCAE will not come up fully (policy handler and service change handler will be stuck at the init stage).

In addition, the Cloudify Manager pod is starting, but not becoming ready.  The stdout/stderr log is interesting, because it does NOT show the final step of actually starting the whole Cloudify system.  The log shows the script copying the database information from the container file system onto a persistent volume but nothing after that.   There should be some file linking after that, and then control is turned over to Cloudify with an /sbin/init command.  It's possible that the log isn't complete, but it's also possible that something is happening with the copy operation.

One thing to check is whether there is a file called "load-plugins.log" in the root directory of the CM container. (Does "/load-plugins.log" exist?).  If so, what does it contain?

I'm using the Wind River environment (but not the same k8s cluster as Vijay), and I saw a successful deployment of patchset 10--with a limited deployment that did not include policy and sdc. (And also not the DCAE policy handler and service change handler.)

I don't think this is the DCAEGEN2-2430 timing issue. (That issue does not prevent CM from becoming ready.  It prevents the bootstrap container from uploading blueprints.)   There might be some
other timing issue involved.

The CM container log on the gating Web site suggests the container never got past the initial steps of setting up a persistent store and never even launched the Cloudify components.   It's possible that the log being displayed on the gating site is incomplete.  That's why I asked about the file called "/load-plugins.log" on the CM container.  If it's *not* present, that would be consistent with the gating site log information.  If it is present, it might provide some clue about why the container is not becoming ready.
